item.title: Gao Zhen, a Chinese Artist Accused of Mocking Mao, Goes on Trial
item.description: Gao Zhen, who emigrated to the United States years ago, was arrested during a visit to China and now faces up to three years in prison for artwork.

item.title: Travel Influencers Share Destinations So Good That They Returned
item.description: Even travel influencers, who spend their lives on the go, have their hearts stolen by certain places.

item.title: How Russia Weaponized the Cold Ukrainian Winter
item.description: Inside one Kyiv neighborhood as it braved the harshest conditions since World War II.

item.title: Iran War Live Updates: Trump Says Iran to Allow More Oil Ships Through Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump characterized the move as a “sign of respect” even as fighting between Israel and Iran continued and the war dragged into its second month.

item.title: Can the ‘Dubai Dream’ Survive the War? Residents Say Life Goes On.
item.description: The Emirates’ largest city pitched itself to foreign workers and tourists as a sun-soaked safe haven in a volatile region. War has challenged that image.

item.title: Myanmar’s Military Leader Moves One Step Closer to Becoming President
item.description: Senior Gen. Min Aung Hlaing stepped down as commander in chief and is expected to be installed as the next civilian leader of the country by its rubber-stamp Parliament.

item.title: Blackouts and hunger grip Cuba
item.description: Residents plagued by power cuts, heaps of rubbish and soaring prices as Donald Trump squeezes communist regime

item.title: Hammering Farage-Trump links could suppress Reform’s poll lead
item.description: Labour and Lib Dems could leverage US president’s unpopularity to hurt momentum behind populist party

item.title: Three killed as unfinished building collapses on church service in Ghana
item.description: Locals say the structure, in a school complex, was in use despite lying unfinished for a number of years.

item.title: Here’s the latest.
In Australia, Victoria and Tasmania Waive Transit Fares as Fuel Prices Soar
item.description: The measures in Victoria and Tasmania are temporary, but show how far and how intensely the oil shock from the war in Iran has spread.

item.title: Oil rises above $115 and Asia shares slide as Iran war enters fifth week
item.description: It comes after Iran-backed Houthi rebels in Yemen joined the conflict by striking Israel over the weekend.

item.title: Remove VAT from energy bills for three years, Tories urge
item.description: There has been a renewed focus on energy costs since the outbreak of the war in Iran.

item.title: How Australia’s seven-month-long manhunt came to an end
item.description: Fugitive Dezi Freeman has been wanted since he shot dead two police officers on his rural property in August last year.

item.title: Welsh Labour pledges income tax freeze at manifesto launch
item.description: Leader Eluned Morgan is due to launch her party's Senedd election manifesto in Swansea.

item.title: Fuel tax halved and free public transport offered as war pushes up prices in Australia
item.description: Victoria and Tasmania are incentivising commuters not to drive in a bid to ease cost pressures.

item.title: Short rates have overshot
item.description: Plus the social media lawsuits

item.title: Myanmar junta chief Min Aung Hlaing nominated as president
item.description: The military leader, who has been sanctioned by Western countries, is now certain to get the job.

item.title: Is THG about to make some mAjOr gAiNs? Over nearly 2,000 words, we struggle to say
item.description: Bulk bye

item.title: G7 crisis talks as Iran war continues to roil global economy
item.description: Also in this newsletter: Drones enter Finnish airspace as Ukraine hits Baltic ports

item.title: FirstFT: Trump says US could ‘take’ Iran oil
item.description: Also in today’s newsletter: Hungary’s low fraud funds recovery rate and ABN Amro’s chief on bank overhaul

item.title: Why does the US have Iran's Kharg Island in its sights?
item.description: The tiny island is home to one of the most critical pieces of Iran's energy infrastructure.

item.title: Asia Depends on L.N.G. From the Middle East. This Is What Happens When It Runs Out.
item.description: With a cutoff in shipments imminent, Asian countries, the biggest importers of liquefied natural gas from the Middle East, are already burning more coal and reducing consumption.

item.title: He Led Congo for 18 Years. Now, Joseph Kabila Is a Hunted Man.
item.description: Joseph Kabila, the former president, faces the death penalty after the government convicted him of treason last year. He says the charges are bogus.

item.title: They’ve Been Accused of Running a ‘Covert’ Operation in Greenland. It’s No Secret.
item.description: Members of President Trump’s circle, working in plain sight, have caught the eye of Denmark’s intelligence services for trying to make friends and cut deals on the Danish territory.

item.title: Private capital: what are the risks?
item.description: As investors seek to retrieve their money, the $22tn industry rejects comparisons with 2008. Regulators aren’t so sure

item.title: WTO reform talks collapse over US-Brazil stand-off
item.description: Dispute over ecommerce tariffs derails agreement on overhaul of global trade body

item.title: UK ministers explore ‘targeted’ energy bill relief for those most in need
item.description: Lower-cost scheme could be delivered through councils under one option being considered

item.title: America’s gig economy
item.description: Self-employment, at both the high and the low end, is keeping consumption afloat — but for how long?

item.title: Eli Lilly pushes for NHS drug price increases in return for its UK investment
item.description: US pharmaceutical group ‘optimistic’ about reaching deal with British government

item.title: Insurers give Emirates ‘outrageously’ cheap war insurance cover
item.description: Dubai-based airline paying additional $100,000 a week while others face far higher charges

item.title: Trapped seafarers suffer as Iran war rages
item.description: Near-closure of Strait of Hormuz has left thousands of ships stuck in the Gulf with limited protections for crew

item.title: Start-up founders shrink from green pitches at progressive SXSW
item.description: Focus shifts from climate-led framing to resilience and risk management

item.title: Food giants were finally starting to cut prices — then the war began
item.description: Spectre of another surge in inflation leaves struggling consumer goods groups with difficult decisions

item.title: US bets billions of dollars on unproven groups in rare earths deals
item.description: Companies with financial ties to Trump administration figures win major funding deals in push for crucial metals

item.title: Exclusive: inside west London’s new go-to dining destination
item.description: After the Olympia exhibition centre’s five-year, £1.3bn redevelopment, an upscale restaurant, bar and events space opens today in its historic Pillar Hall. We had a first look

item.title: Hungary returns just 18% of funds flagged by EU fraud watchdog
item.description: Viktor Orbán’s government has returned just 18 per cent of funds flagged by the EU’s anti-graft body

item.title: The karaoke company, the penny stock investor and the $17bn trucking rout
item.description: Controversial trader John Fife invested in Algorhythm months before its AI announcement triggered a surge in its share price and a market panic

item.title: How Trump's tariffs ripped up the global trade order
item.description: In the first installment of a new FTNB series this week, we explore how President Trump’s so-called Liberation Day has shaken up the global trade order

item.title: New U.S. Missile Hit Iranian Sports Hall and School, Analysis Shows
item.description: The Pentagon used missiles untested in combat in a deadly attack that struck civilian sites near a military compound on Feb. 28, according to visual evidence examined by The Times and weapons experts.

item.title: U.S. Allows Russian Oil Tanker to Reach Cuba, Despite Blockade
item.description: The tanker full of crude oil could reach its expected destination by Monday, providing a lifeline to the island amid intense U.S. pressure.

item.title: Latin Patriarch will have access to Jerusalem holy site after police stopped entry
item.description: The Israeli PM said Cardinal Pierbattista Pizzaballa had been asked not to enter the church out of concern for his safety.

item.title: Why Chinese tech companies are racing to set up in Hong Kong
item.description: Mainland firms are using the territory to test products and as a springboard for global expansion.

item.title: Thieves steal Renoir, Cézanne and Matisse paintings in three-minute Italian heist
item.description: The paintings were taken from a museum in Parma by four masked men in the middle of the night, police say.

item.title: Educated and employed but still struggling: India's middle class under strain
item.description: Once aspirational, India's middle class today are finding fewer avenues for their ambition.

item.title: Italy investigates Sephora and Benefit over marketing skincare to children
item.description: The Italian Competition Authority said luxury goods giant LVMH appears to have adopted "particularly insidious" marketing strategies.

item.title: Can Canada’s Left Regroup? A New Leader Will Try.
item.description: Seeking a comeback after voters flocked to Mark Carney’s Liberals, the New Democrats picked Avi Lewis, a scion of a leftist political dynasty.

item.title: Councils pressed to use universal parking app to cut 'unfair' fines
item.description: The National Parking Platform already has 15 councils on board with more in talks to join.

item.title: All sides in the Gulf war are at risk of overplaying their hands
item.description: The costs of dragging out the conflict could be very high indeed

item.title: The Slum in Gangnam, the Richest Part of Seoul
item.description: The city wants to redevelop a shantytown in Gangnam district, where hundreds are defying eviction, fighting for a right to own a home in an area notorious for the exorbitant cost of housing.

item.title: Is Bollywood’s latest megahit propaganda for Narendra Modi?
item.description: “Dhurandhar: The Revenge” portrays India extracting a price for Pakistani perfidy

item.title: Three arrested in Paris after attempted bomb attack outside Bank of America
item.description: France's anti-terrorism prosecutor's office said it had immediately taken over the investigation.

item.title: Hundreds in Beirut mourn journalists killed in Israeli strike
item.description: BBC reports from the funerals of three journalists killed by a targeted attack in southern Lebanon.

item.title: Caf general secretary resigns amid Afcon final fallout
item.description: Veron Mosengo-Omba, a controversial figure, leaves at a turbulent time for African football.

item.title: 'I was naive,' says minister who quit over Labour Together claims
item.description: Josh Simons resigned after facing claims a think tank he used to run commissioned a report into journalists' backgrounds.

item.title: China’s leadership is about to be shaken up
item.description: The changes will affect every level. Except one

item.title: Why are Scotland playing Ivory Coast in Liverpool?
item.description: Scotland continue their World Cup preparations with a friendly against Ivory Coast on Tuesday, but why is this away game being played at Everton's new stadium in Liverpool?

item.title: Cut taxes on energy bills before giving bailouts, Badenoch says
item.description: The Tory leader refuses to rule out direct payments to households if bills spike but says this would come at a cost.

item.title: Schools do not have enough staff to make SEND reforms work, union warns
item.description: The National Education Union says schools need more funding to be able to make all classrooms inclusive.

item.title: A Toothless Iran? Missile and Drone Strikes Show It Can Still Inflict Pain.
item.description: A wave of strikes across the Middle East in recent days shows that Iran has not lost the capacity to retaliate.

item.title: Halfway Through Lent, a Small Quebec Island Celebrates With Masks and Jigs
item.description: Few islanders still observe Lent, but they cling to a tradition once seen as defying the all-powerful Roman Catholic Church.

item.title: One ant for $220: The new frontier of wildlife trafficking
item.description: The craze for collecting ants takes Kenya by surprise as smugglers zone in to make a profit.

item.title: How deepfake porn scandal surrounding TV star rocked Germany
item.description: Collien Fernandes has accused her ex-husband of spreading images of her online, but he has categorically denied it.

item.title: India restoring a centuries-old royal kitchen that never stopped serving food
item.description: The royal kitchen still serves food, but the complex has been worn down by time.

item.title: Shops and restaurants in Egypt told to close early as energy crisis deepens
item.description: Retail and dining premises will have to shut by 21:00 each night for the next month to conserve power.

item.title: In Dubai, a famed horse race goes on, despite the war.
The Houthis’ attack on Israel heralds escalation of the Iran war
item.description: Yemen’s Shia militia could threaten to choke the Red Sea

item.title: Iran-backed Houthis join war with attack against Israel
item.description: There are fears the Iranian proxy may attack shipping in the Red Sea, adding to global economic woes.

item.title: Ukraine Finalizes Air Defense Deals With Gulf Nations Amid War in Mideast
item.description: The Ukrainian president visited the Middle East, where he sought to shift his country from a recipient of military aid to a supplier.

item.title: On Iran’s Rugged Frontier, Kurds Yearn to Join the Fight
item.description: These groups’ dream has long been to establish federal autonomy, akin to that of their fellow Kurds in Iraq. With Iran’s leaders battered and degraded, they hope their moment has come.

item.title: Two Cuba-bound aid ships found days after disappearing
item.description: The crew are safe and will complete their mission, a spokesman for the organisers said.

item.title: Potential Houthi threat to Red Sea shipping could further damage global economy
item.description: The Iran-backed group could bring a second crucial waterway to a standstill, writes Sebastian Usher.

item.title: Tears and defiance as Lebanese family bury child, 11, killed in Israeli strike
item.description: Jawad Younes and his uncle Ragheb were laid to rest on Saturday after their family compound was hit a day earlier.

item.title: Senegal parade Afcon trophy despite title controversy
item.description: Senegal parade the Africa Cup of Nations trophy before Saturday's friendly match against Peru in Paris - despite being stripped of the title earlier this month.

item.title: Twenty-two migrants die off Greek coast after six days at sea
item.description: Poor weather and a lack of food and water contributed to the deaths, the Greek coastguard says.

item.title: Nepal's ex-PM arrested over fatal protest crackdown
item.description: More than 70 people were killed during an uprising last year which ousted Oli's government.

item.title: Gaza peace doubts deepen as attention shifts to Iran
item.description: With the world focused on the Iran war, there is increasing uncertainty about what happens next for Gaza.

item.title: 'Our home is gone': BBC speaks to displaced families in Lebanon
item.description: The BBC's Hugo Bachega speaks to families in Beirut as more than a million people have been displaced in Lebanon.

item.title: A Mamdani Strategist’s Advice for Democrats in the 2026 Midterms
item.description: How to talk about affordability.

item.title: Video shows US missile likely used in deadly strike on Iranian town, experts say
item.description: The analysts identified a Precision Strike Missile (PrSM) in footage of the strike on the southern town of Lamerd.

item.title: From jammed broadcasts to a blocked website: BBC Russian's 80 years of defiance
item.description: For half a century, BBC Russian had to fend off jammers. Now its website is blocked, but millions still read it.

item.title: 'The gravest crime against humanity': What does the UN vote on slavery mean?
item.description: African and Caribbean nations want countries which benefited from slavery to pay compensation but it will not be straightforward.

item.title: Maldives tells UK it does not recognise Chagos Islands deal
item.description: The Maldives is threatening international legal action to press its claim for control of the Chagos Islands.

item.title: 'Memory of a generation': China mourns the sudden death of a controversial education influencer
item.description: The divisive 41-year-old was among the country's most prominent influencers.

item.title: I escaped North Korea with my mum. Now I'm terrified she might be sent back
item.description: Geumseong survived a dangerous journey to South Korea, but his mother is stuck in a Chinese prison and may be forcibly repatriated.

item.title: 'My daughter is under the rubble': Inside Tehran as civilian toll of strikes rises
item.description: One month since the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran, Tehran residents tell the BBC their lives have been devastated.

item.title: Offord says he is fit to be first minister after homophobic joke
item.description: Malcolm Offord denied being homophobic after apologising for a joke he made about George Michael during a Burns Night speech.

item.title: Screen time for under-fives should be limited to one hour a day, parents told
item.description: New government guidance also suggests children under-two should not be watching screens alone.

item.title: Austria becomes latest to propose social media ban for children
item.description: The proposed under-14 ban follows similar steps in other European countries, including France and Spain.

item.title: No evidence of 'family voting' in by-election
item.description: An investigation into alleged "family voting" finds no evidence of intent to influence voting.

item.title: Salah could play for seven more years - Klopp
item.description: Jurgen Klopp says Mohamed Salah could carry on playing until the age of 40 after the forward leaves Liverpool at the end of the season.

item.title: Reform candidate quits Senedd election after appearing to do Nazi salute
item.description: Corey Edwards and party leader Nigel Farage suggested he had been imitating Fawlty Towers character Basil Fawlty.

item.title: Vital paperwork for grooming gangs inquiry may be lost, say MPs
item.description: An independent inquiry into child sexual abuse by grooming gangs is about to begin its work.

item.title: Reform councillor suspended following racism row
item.description: Reform UK confirms the suspension of Lynn Dean and says an investigation is forthcoming.

item.title: Why did US and Israel attack Iran and how long could the war last?
item.description: Conflict has spread across the Middle East since the US and Israel launched strikes on Iran on 28 February.

item.title: Whale swims for freedom after big German rescue effort on Baltic coast
item.description: Rescue teams now believe the whale has reached deeper water in Lübeck Bay and hope it will head for the wider sea.

item.title: Thirteen arrested in joint Scotland and Spain gangland raids
item.description: Police Scotland and the Guardia Civil in Spain carried out simultaneous raids as part of a two-year probe into organised crime.

item.title: Assisted dying bill will not now become law, say both sides
item.description: The House of Lords are continuing to debate the proposals but there is not enough time to complete all the stages in the current parliamentary session.

item.title: UN human rights chief calls on US to conclude probe into Iran school strike
item.description: The strike - which killed at least 168 people, mostly children - "evoked a visceral horror", Volker Türk said.

item.title: Is Trump's pause on attacking Iranian energy for diplomacy or an escalation?
item.description: The US president's commitment to deadlines is fluid but he uses them for a purpose, writes the BBC's James Landale.

item.title: Officials to ask Mandelson to hand over personal phone messages for release
item.description: Officials are set to publish more documents related to his appointment as UK ambassador to the US - but have so far only had access to the peer's work phone.

item.title: Palestinian man killed as death toll from West Bank settler violence climbs
item.description: Mohammad al-Malhi is the seventh Palestinian killed by a settler in a surge of violence since the start of the war with Iran.

item.title: Spanish woman who died through euthanasia failed by state, say critics
item.description: Noelia Castillo died on Thursday evening in a Barcelona hospital, after a protracted legal battle with her own father.

item.title: Davey defends councils raising charges and taxes
item.description: Cost of living is a key campaign issue for the Liberal Democrats ahead of May's local elections.

item.title: Morocco ready to host Wafcon 'any time' as questions remain over postponement
item.description: A senior member of the Moroccan government says the country was “ready to host” the Women’s Africa Cup of Nations this month despite the decision to delay it until July.

item.title: Rationing power and diluting petrol - how African countries are coping with effects of Iran war
item.description: The disruption in oil supplies has caused shortages and concerns in several African countries.

item.title: Ukraine signs deal with Saudi Arabia offering drone expertise
item.description: Volodymyr Zelensky says the Gulf kingdom faces the same type of attacks from Iran that Kyiv has been resisting.
